text: A liver resection, or partial hepatectomy, is a surgical procedure performed for the treatment of tumors in the liver. The type of liver resection we perform depends on the size and location of the tumor within the liver.

Many of these surgeries must be performed in a traditional way through a large incision underneath the right ribs. At TriHealth, we occasionally are able to perform this surgery minimally invasively, either laparoscopically or with the use of robotic technology.

The following video is of a real minimally invasive partial hepatectomy performed at TriHealth. This video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical hepatectomy. The purpose of this video is to give patients an idea of how this surgery is performed. If you are scheduled to have this procedure, your surgery may be performed in a similar fashion.
The robotic instruments are positioned within the surgical field. On the left side of the screen, the normal liver tissue is visible, with the tumor attached to or growing from it.
The surgeon identifies adhesions between the liver tumor and the omentum, a fatty layer hanging from the stomach, preparing to separate these attachments.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer, an energy device, to divide the attachments between the tumor and the omentum. The device seals the tissue with heat and then cuts through it with an integrated blade, freeing the tumor from the omentum.

The liver is visible on the upper left side of the screen, exhibiting a yellowish hue indicative of fatty changes, known as non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), contrasting with the typical brown color of a normal liver.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to drop the liver, revealing attachments between the liver and the diaphragm that need to be dissected to remove the tumor. The robotic approach allows for precise manipulation at severe angles, enhancing visibility and access compared to traditional laparoscopic techniques.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery, shaped like a hook, to apply heat energy for dividing and cauterizing tissue attachments between the liver and diaphragm. This step is necessary to mobilize the liver for safe tumor removal.

The surgeon displays the normal liver on the left side of the screen, with the tumor visibly growing from it. A scoring line is marked on the liver to guide the division, ensuring complete removal of the tumor.

The liver can have multiple types of tumors. The most common tumor that the liver gets is actually metastatic cancer from another organ. And the most common of that would be colorectal cancer. So colon cancer or rectal cancer that started in the colon or rectum, but then has spread or metastasized to the liver. There are other types of tumors in the liver, some of which that aren't even cancers. We usually don't remove those unless they're so large that they cause symptoms.

The surgeon uses a harmonic HD scalpel to divide the scored portion of the liver, aiming to minimize bleeding due to the liver's vascular nature. The scalpel is applied to the liver's surface and the initial centimeters of tissue, carefully cutting through the liver to ensure complete removal of the tumor.

The surgeon identifies larger blood vessels deeper within the liver tissue, preparing to address them using a surgical stapler. The liver is visually divided into eight segments based on its blood supply and venous drainage.

The surgeon methodically dissects through the liver parenchyma, starting at one end and progressing shallowly across the tissue, resembling the movement of a typewriter. Upon reaching the end, the process is repeated from the beginning, gradually deepening the dissection.

The liver parenchyma has been divided, revealing large blood vessels that require management with a vascular stapler. The stapler articulates to bend in different directions, allowing precise angling to divide the tissue correctly. Multiple firings may be necessary to completely free the specimen.

The surgeon completes the final firing of the vascular stapler, fully freeing the liver specimen. The cut surface of the liver is visible, and the surgeon uses an argon beam to cauterize the edge, sealing exposed blood vessels and bile ducts to prevent postoperative bleeding and bile leaks.
The surgeon places the liver specimen into a retrieval bag for removal, preparing to send it to pathology for margin confirmation following the partial hepatectomy.
